question_text: Let 0, 1, 1, 2, 0 be five observations of a random variable X which follows a Poisson distribution with parameter $\theta > 0 .$ . Let the minimum variance unbiased estimate of $P ( X \leq 1 )$ , based on this data, be $\alpha .$ . Then $5 ^ { 4 } \alpha$ (in integer) is equal to
solution:
For i.i.d. Poisson(θ), the sufficient and complete statistic is the total $\begin{array} { r } { T = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 5 } X _ { i } \sim \operatorname { P o i s s o n } ( 5 \theta ) . } \end{array}$

 $ h ( T ) = \mathbb { E } \big [ \mathbf { 1 } \{ X _ { 1 } \leq 1 \} \mid T \big ] $ 

 $ g ( \theta ) = P ( X \leq 1 ) = e ^ { - \theta } ( 1 + \theta ) $ 

 $ X _ { 1 } \mid T = t \sim $ 

 $ T = t , $ 

 $ h ( t ) = P ( X _ { 1 } \leq 1 \mid T = t ) = \left( { \frac { 4 } { 5 } } \right) ^ { t } + { \frac { t } { 5 } } \left( { \frac { 4 } { 5 } } \right) ^ { t - 1 } . $ 

With the observed data, $t = \sum ( 0 , 1 , 1 , 2 , 0 ) = 4 \mathrm { { ; } }$ ; therefore

 $ \alpha = h ( 4 ) = \left( { \frac { 4 } { 5 } } \right) ^ { 4 } + { \frac { 4 } { 5 } } \left( { \frac { 4 } { 5 } } \right) ^ { 3 } = { \frac { 2 5 6 } { 6 2 5 } } + { \frac { 2 5 6 } { 6 2 5 } } = { \frac { 5 1 2 } { 6 2 5 } } . $ 

Thus,

 $ 5 ^ { 4 } \alpha = 6 2 5 \times \frac { 5 1 2 } { 6 2 5 } = 5 1 2 . $ 
Quick Tip

For Poisson samples, obtain UMVUEs by conditioning on the complete sufficient statistic $T = \sum X _ { i }$ and using Poisson–multinomial splitting.
